text: When working with APIs, you first make HTTP requests—usually GET to retrieve data or POST to send it. The response commonly comes in JSON format, which is easy to parse in most programming languages. Many APIs require authentication, often via an API key included in the request headers, to verify your identity. However, you must also respect rate limits, which restrict how many requests you can make in a given time. If you exceed these limits, your requests may be blocked. Finally, always handle errors gracefully: check the HTTP status code and implement fallback logic so your application doesn't crash when an API call fails.
